region_of_origin: India (Punjab / North India); widely adopted in South Asia and globally

ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for chicken tikka masala as a prepared dish; SNOMED CT coverage of composite curry dishes is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Chicken Tikka Masala in Singapore is approximately 300 g, comprising grilled chicken pieces in a rich spiced gravy, usually accompanied by naan or rice ordered separately.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken tikka masala, restaurant-style — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 revised edition) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for chicken tikka masala (NDB 35193)
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN); USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary values adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) for chicken curry with cream-based gravy, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy composite entry for chicken tikka masala (NDB 35193). Per-100 g values represent the curry dish without accompanying bread or rice. Sodium reflects restaurant-level seasoning typical of North Indian restaurant preparations in Singapore.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed Indian dishes and curry-based preparations — analogous estimate based on chicken curry with cream gravy; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for chicken tikka masala was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on the closest analogous entry: chicken curry with cream-based gravy, which is a low-protein-and-fat-dominant dish with moderate carbohydrate from onion, tomato, and cream. The low GI reflects the fat and protein content of the dish slowing gastric emptying and glucose absorption. Glycemic load per 300 g serving calculated as (35 × 18.6 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 6.5 (low GL).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from restaurant-level seasoning (~420 mg per 100 g; ~1260 mg per 300 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 150–200 g and request reduced-salt preparation, as a full restaurant serving can contribute over 50% of the recommended daily sodium intake of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~35) due to high protein, fat, and spice content moderating glucose absorption
type2_diabetes.guidance: Chicken Tikka Masala has a low glycemic index and moderate glycemic load per serving, making it a relatively suitable choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-GI accompaniments such as white rice or plain naan; pairing with whole-wheat roti or cauliflower rice is preferred.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from cream and ghee (~3.1 g per 100 g) and elevated sodium in restaurant preparations
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should be mindful of the saturated fat contributed by cream and ghee in restaurant-style preparations; requesting a lighter cream or yoghurt-based version and limiting portion to 200 g can help keep saturated fat intake within the recommended 20 g daily limit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken Tikka Masala is a North Indian curry dish consisting of boneless chicken pieces that are first marinated in spiced yoghurt and grilled in a tandoor (clay oven) to produce charred, smoky tikka, then simmered in a rich, velvety gravy made from onions, tomatoes, cream, ghee, and a blend of aromatic spices including garam masala, cumin, coriander, turmeric, chilli, ginger, garlic, and kasuri methi (dried fenugreek leaves). The dish has a deep orange-red colour, a creamy and mildly tangy flavour profile, and a tender, slightly smoky chicken texture. It is one of the most popular North Indian restaurant dishes globally and is widely served in Singapore's Indian restaurants, particularly along South Bridge Road and Serangoon Road. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 142 kcal, 12.5 g protein, 7.8 g fat (of which 3.1 g is saturated), and 6.2 g carbohydrate, with a sodium content of around 420 mg reflecting restaurant-level seasoning. The dish has a low glycemic index (estimated ~35) due to its high protein and fat content, making it relatively suitable for individuals managing blood glucose. However, the sodium and saturated fat content from cream and ghee warrant moderation for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. It is typically served with naan, roti, or basmati rice. The dish is halal-certified in most Singapore North Indian restaurants. It is also known as Murgh Tikka Masala in Hindi and is sometimes abbreviated as CTM.
common_user_queries: How many calories in chicken tikka masala?
Is chicken tikka masala good for diabetics?
What is the GI of chicken tikka masala?
Is chicken tikka masala high in sodium?
What spices are in chicken tikka masala?
Is chicken tikka masala halal in Singapore?
Chicken tikka masala vs butter chicken — what is the difference?
How much protein in chicken tikka masala?
meal_context: served with naan or basmati rice

confidence_reason: Nutrient values adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy composite entry for chicken tikka masala. No single exact Singapore restaurant-specific laboratory analysis was available; values represent a well-supported adaptation from the two closest approved primary sources. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework, not a direct measurement for this dish.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are per 100 g of the curry dish only, excluding naan or rice. 2. Sodium value (~420 mg/100 g) reflects typical restaurant-level seasoning in Singapore North Indian restaurants; home-prepared versions may be significantly lower. 3. Vitamin D is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN or USDA SR Legacy source entries for this dish. 4. GI is an analogous estimate (35, low) based on the closest approved GI source entry for cream-based chicken curry; no direct GI measurement for chicken tikka masala was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. 5. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy fatty acid profiles for similar chicken curry preparations. 6. The dish is described as 'spicy' and 'chef recommendation' in the field survey listing; these are menu descriptors and do not alter the nutritional classification. 7. FoodEx2 code A0CKM and FoodOn URI FOODON_03311069 are used as the best available ontology anchors; reviewers should verify these codes against the latest FoodEx2 and FoodOn releases. 8. Trans fat is reported as 0.1 g per 100 g reflecting minor naturally occurring trans fats from dairy (cream, ghee); this is not an industrial trans fat source.
